questions.question_text: A first-time buyer with moderate income wants help with a down payment. Which California state agency specifically offers subordinate loans for down payment assistance?
distractors: CalVet
DFPI
Cal-OSBA
questions.explanation: The California Housing Finance Agency (CalHFA) offers first mortgage programs at below-market rates and subordinate (second) loan programs for down payment and closing cost assistance for first-time and low-to-moderate income buyers.

questions.question_text: A California veteran wants to buy a home using a CalVet loan. Which statement accurately describes how CalVet financing works?
questions.correct_answer: CalVet purchases the property and sells it to the veteran on a land contract
distractors: CalVet guarantees a VA loan made by a private lender
CalVet provides a grant covering up to 20% of the purchase price
CalVet co-signs the veteran's conventional mortgage
questions.explanation: CalVet's unique structure involves the California Department of Veterans Affairs purchasing the property outright and then selling it to the veteran through a land contract (contract of sale). The veteran gains equitable title while CalVet retains legal title until the loan is paid off.

questions.question_text: A buyer is comparing CalHFA and CalVet programs. She served in the California National Guard. Which program is she most likely eligible for?
questions.correct_answer: CalVet, because it serves California veterans including National Guard members
distractors: Only CalHFA, because CalVet is for active-duty only
Neither program covers National Guard service
Both programs require federal VA eligibility as a prerequisite
questions.explanation: CalVet serves California veterans, which includes members of the National Guard who have met service requirements. CalHFA is available to first-time buyers regardless of veteran status. The two programs serve different target populations.

questions.question_text: A first-time buyer uses a CalHFA first mortgage combined with a CalHFA subordinate loan for her down payment. Which statement best describes the subordinate loan?
questions.correct_answer: It is a second loan subordinate to the first, repaid per its own terms
distractors: It must be repaid within 5 years with no interest
It is forgiven automatically after 10 years of occupancy
It reduces the purchase price rather than providing cash
questions.explanation: CalHFA subordinate programs provide second mortgage financing for down payment and closing costs. The second loan is subordinate to (behind) the first mortgage in lien priority and is repaid according to its own specific program terms.

questions.question_text: A first-time buyer with moderate income is comparing CalHFA to a conventional loan. What is CalHFA's primary advantage?
questions.correct_answer: Below-market interest rates and down payment help
distractors: No income limits or credit score requirements
Zero closing costs on all CalHFA loan products
Guaranteed approval regardless of debt-to-income ratio
questions.explanation: CalHFA's key benefit is offering below-market interest rates plus subordinate loans for down payment and closing cost assistance. It targets first-time and low-to-moderate income buyers who might not qualify for conventional financing terms.

questions.question_text: A private individual in California wants to lend money at 15% interest on a personal note, not arranged through a broker. Is this permitted under California usury law?
questions.correct_answer: No, the rate exceeds California's constitutional usury limit for non-exempt lenders
distractors: Yes, private parties can charge any interest rate they agree to
Yes, but only on loans secured by real property
No, the limit for all private loans is 7% per year
questions.explanation: For loans used primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, the California Constitution caps interest at 10% per year for non-exempt lenders; other loans get the greater of 10% or 5% plus the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco's discount rate. A 15% personal note from a private individual exceeds the cap either way. (7% is only the default rate when no rate is agreed.)

questions.question_text: Which of the following lenders is EXEMPT from California's usury interest rate limits?
questions.correct_answer: A licensed California real estate broker arranging a loan
distractors: A private individual making a direct loan without a broker involved
An unlicensed hard money lender lending its own funds
A family trust lending directly to an unrelated borrower
questions.explanation: Licensed real estate brokers who make or arrange loans secured by real property are exempt from California's constitutional usury limits, as are banks, savings institutions, credit unions, and licensed finance lenders. Private parties lending directly — with no licensed broker arranging the loan — remain subject to the cap. (Seller carryback financing sits outside the usury law entirely: a credit sale is not a 'loan' under the time-price doctrine.)

questions.question_text: A credit union in California makes a real estate loan at 18% interest. A borrower claims this violates the usury limit. Is the borrower correct?
questions.correct_answer: No, credit unions are exempt from California usury restrictions
distractors: Yes, 18% exceeds the 10% constitutional cap for all lenders
Yes, unless the Federal Reserve rate exceeds 13%
No, but only if the loan is secured by residential property
questions.explanation: Credit unions, along with banks, savings associations, and licensed brokers, are specifically exempt from California's constitutional usury interest rate limits. These institutional lenders may charge rates set by market conditions and their regulatory frameworks.

questions.question_text: A private individual lends money at 12% interest, not arranged through a broker. The borrower claims usury. Which factor determines whether the rate is legal?
questions.correct_answer: Whether the rate exceeds California's constitutional cap
distractors: Whether the borrower signed a usury waiver
Whether the loan is secured by commercial property
Whether the loan term exceeds five years
questions.explanation: California's usury law sets a constitutional interest rate ceiling for non-exempt lenders. Private individuals lending outside the exempt categories (banks, brokers, credit unions) are subject to this cap. The rate, not the loan term or property type, determines legality.

questions.question_text: A real estate broker arranges a hard money loan for a client at 14% interest. Under California's Real Property Loan Law, what document must the broker provide to the borrower?
distractors: Good Faith Estimate (GFE)
HUD-1 Settlement Statement
Affiliated Business Arrangement Disclosure
questions.explanation: Under California's Real Property Loan Law (B&PC §10240+), brokers arranging real property loans must provide a Mortgage Loan Disclosure Statement (MLDS), also called the Broker Loan Statement (§10241). This discloses costs, commissions, and loan terms before commitment.

questions.question_text: California's Real Property Loan Law (B&PC §10240+) applies to which type of lending activity?
questions.correct_answer: Broker-arranged loans secured by real property
distractors: All loans made by banks and credit unions
Only purchase-money loans under $250,000
VA and FHA loans originated by licensed lenders
questions.explanation: The Real Property Loan Law governs loans arranged by real estate brokers that are secured by real property. It imposes disclosure requirements (MLDS), maximum commission limits based on loan term and amount, and predatory lending protections.

questions.question_text: A broker arranges a hard money loan on a small residential property. Which factor determines the maximum commission the broker can charge under the Real Property Loan Law?
questions.correct_answer: The loan term and loan amount combined
distractors: The broker's negotiated rate with the borrower
The lender's stated yield requirement
Market rates in the county where the property is located
questions.explanation: Under California's Real Property Loan Law, maximum broker commissions on loans secured by real property are set by statute based on two factors: the loan term (short vs. long) and the loan amount (smaller loans have higher relative caps). Brokers cannot exceed these statutory maximums.

questions.question_text: A California borrower complains that a broker charged fees far exceeding any loan benefit. Which law is most directly designed to address this type of predatory lending concern?
distractors: California Homeowner Bill of Rights
California Financial Code §22000
Holden Act
questions.explanation: California's Real Property Loan Law specifically includes consumer protections against predatory practices in broker-arranged loans, including caps on costs and commissions. It was designed to protect borrowers from excessive charges in private or hard money lending.

questions.question_text: A borrower signs an MLDS prepared by a broker before receiving any loan funds. What key information must the MLDS disclose?
questions.correct_answer: Broker's costs, estimated loan charges, and loan terms
distractors: The lender's internal credit scoring model
The property's current market value appraisal
Borrower's credit score and debt-to-income ratio
questions.explanation: The Mortgage Loan Disclosure Statement (MLDS) must disclose the broker's compensation, estimated costs, loan terms, and other material information about the loan. It must be delivered before the borrower commits to the loan so they can make an informed decision.

questions.question_text: A licensed California real estate broker arranges a loan at 22% for a wealthy investor's commercial project. Another broker argues this violates the usury law. Who is correct?
questions.correct_answer: The arranging broker is correct — licensed brokers are exempt from usury limits
distractors: The second broker is correct — 22% exceeds the 10% cap for all loans
Neither — commercial loans have a separate 15% usury cap
The second broker is correct unless the borrower waived usury protections in writing
questions.explanation: Licensed California real estate brokers are exempt from the state's constitutional usury limits. A broker can arrange a real property loan at any rate the market will support. The exemption applies regardless of loan size or property type.
